entry.title: commission /kəˈmɪʃn/
entry.content:
noun, verb

  • 回扣
    You get a 10% commission on everything you sell.
  • 委员会
  • 正式委托/军官任命
    She has been commissioned to write a new national anthem.
  • 犯罪
    the commission of a crime

  • 可/不可使用 in/out of commission

    Several of the airline's planes are temporarily out of commission and undergoing safety checks.

entry.title: proximity /prɑːkˈsɪməti/
entry.content:
• in close proximity of sth to sth
    （formal) the state of being near sth/sb in distance or time

    The area has a number of schools in close proximity to each other

  • proximity effect 邻近效应

  • proximity sensor 近距离传感器

entry.title: azimuth /ˈæzɪməθ/
entry.content:
方位角
An azimuth is an angular measurement in a spherical coordinate system.

entry.title: dispute
entry.content:
verb /dɪˈspjuːt/

  • to question whether sth is true and valid
  • to argue or disagree strongly with sb about sth, especially about who owns sth
  • dispute something: to fight to get control of sth or win sth

These figures have been disputed.

No one is disputed that there is a problem.

It is disputed whether the law applies in this case.

noun /ˈdɪspjuːt/

  • dispute with
  • dispute over
  • dispute between
  • be beyond dispute
    everyone agrees that it is true or it really happened.
  • be open to dispute
  • be in dispute

entry.title: wobble /ˈwɑːbl/
entry.content:
verb

  • to move from side to side in an unsteady way

    This chair wobbles.
    Her voice wobbled with emotion.
    He wobbled off his bike.

  • to hesitate or lose confidence about doing something
    Yesterday the president showed the first signs of wobbling over the issue.

entry.title: heuristic /hjʊ'rɪstɪk/
entry.content:
启发式的，探索式的
A commonly used heuristic procedure to estimate the effect of uncertainty arising from choices made in parameterization and regularization is to perform repeated inversions with these factors varied between runs.

entry.title: nuisance
entry.content:
讨厌的人，麻烦的事

He could be a bit of a nuisance when he was drunk.

I don't want to be a nuisance so tell me if you want to be alone.

Stop making a nuisance of yourself.

  • (law)
    He was charged with causinng a public nuisance.

entry.title: indulge /ɪnˈdʌldʒ/
entry.content:
verb
沉溺；放纵；纵容

They went into town to to indulge in some serious shopping.

She was free to indulge in a little romantic daydreaming.

The inheritance enables him to indulge his passion for art.

His questions were annoying but is was easier to indulge him than try and protest.

She did not believe in indulging the children with presents.

entry.title: novel /ˈnɑːvl/
entry.content:
noun 小说
adjective 新颖的 new

He came up with the novel idea of drive-in restaurants.

entry.title: deficiency /dɪˈfɪʃnsi/
entry.content:
shortage

  • the state of not having, or not having enough of, sth that is essential
  • a fault or a weakness in sth/sb that makes it or them less successful

entry.title: exploit
entry.content:
负面

  • to treat someone unfairly by asking them to do things for you, but giving them very little in return.
  • to try to get as much as you can out of a situation, sometimes unfairly.

正面

  • to use something fully and effectively.
    The new TV companies are fully exploiting the potential of satellite transmission.

中性

  • to develop and use minerals, forests, oil etc for business or industry.

entry.title: circuit /ˈsɜːrkɪt/
entry.content:
noun

  • a line, route or journey around a place 环行/环线
    The earth takes a year to make a circuit of the sun.
  • 电路
    integrated circuit 集成电路
    printed circuit 印刷电路
    short circuit 短路
    analog circuit 模拟电路
  • 巡回 sports/lecture/court/judge

entry.title: paradigm, /ˈpærədaɪm/
entry.content:
noun

  • 典范
    He had become the paradigm of the successful man.

  • 范式 a model or example that shows how sth works or is produced; a very clear or typical example of something

entry.title: retrieval，/rɪˈtriːvl/
entry.content:
uncountable noun

  • 取回 (formal) the process of getting sth back, especially from a place where it should not be

    The ship was buried, beyond retrieval, at the bottom of the sea.
    (无法挽回)

  • 数据检索 information retrieval

entry.title: derive, /dɪˈraɪv/
entry.content:
verb

  • 起源于，来自于
    This word is derived from Latin.

    Our theory was derived from Kruschev's Theory of Darkness.

  • 获得，取得，得到 to get something, especially an advantage or pleasant feeling, from something

    Many students derived enormous satisfaction from the course.

    It is a demanding job, but he derives a lot of satisfaction from it.

    Most of the companies had derived positive benefits from their investment.

  • 从公式推出；从化学方程式获得；文章得出的结论
    We emphasize the need to assume uniformity of extended preferences among individual observes; otherwise, the ordinary utilitarian formula cannot be derives.

    This assumption does not affect the qualitative conclusions derived in the paper.
